notes.title: GPU price tracker plan
notes.body:
Goal: build a small service to monitor daily GPU prices across several retailers and alert when price-per-performance crosses a threshold.

Architecture: a Python fetcher running on a schedule, with request concurrency tuned to meet retailer TOS and keep latency under 800 ms per page. Data sources: retailer HTML pages and a public pricing API that requires an API key stored securely in a local .env file (or in the CI secret store for deployments). We will normalize data into a tiny SQLite DB, compute moving averages, and export a lightweight dashboard.

Risks: blocking, captcha challenges, and flaky page structures. Mitigations: randomized backoff, rotating user agents, robust parsing with schema checks, and graceful degradation when a field is missing. Success metric: median end-to-end latency under 2s including parsing and writes; alert delivery within 5 min of price change detection. Stretch goal: add simple price anomaly detection with a rolling z-score.

notes.title: Backyard garden irrigation ideas
notes.body:
Exploration of an automated drip irrigation system for the backyard garden.

Sensors: soil moisture and ambient temperature; optional rain sensor. Controller: ESP32 with Wi‑Fi, deep sleep between readings. Control loop: open valve for 2–5 minutes when moisture is below threshold and no rain is detected; pause during cool evenings to reduce mildew risk.

Design considerations: multi‑zone layout (herbs, tomatoes, peppers), quick‑connects to simplify maintenance, and a manual override switch. Integration ideas: Home Assistant for dashboards and notifications; seasonal presets to reduce watering in shoulder seasons. This remains a weekend experiment, but it would save time and improve plant health.

notes.title: DDIA book takeaways
notes.body:
Notes from Designing Data‑Intensive Applications (Kleppmann).

The book emphasizes trade‑offs between consistency, availability, and latency. Streaming systems with exactly‑once semantics are notoriously hard; idempotent consumers and deterministic processing simplify reprocessing. Storage engines: LSM‑tree vs B‑Tree and their write/read trade‑offs. Replication models and conflict resolution strategies (e.g., last‑write‑wins vs CRDTs) depend on business needs and user expectations.

Data evolution: forward/backward compatibility, schema migration strategies, and feature flags to decouple deployment from release. A recurring theme: understand tail latency and apply backpressure to prevent overload cascades.
